i just heard the term "voice driven development" from a strangeloop talk <@U5TCAFTD3>  posted....but we've been following that paradigm for our new languages- stick to words and not syntax characters whenever possible so you could in theory dictate a program quite naturally, and it seems to be a good trend....if you assume an advanced editor, then the keystroke savings you get from using punctuation disappear (because of autocomplete, for example) and readability and accessibility and maintability increase

Congrats on the beta release to the dark team!
I have a couple of questions:
- Can a project have a `staging` and `production` databases?
- Are `all` requests saved? What happen when your project has been running for months and you have thousands of requests?
I would love give a try to the editor!

Congrats on the beta release to the dark team!
I have a couple of questions:
- Can a project have a `staging` and `production` databases?
- Are `all` requests saved? What happen when your project has been running for months and you have thousands of requests?
I would love give a try to the editor!

A feature I can imagine useful is tagging functions like in Smalltalk.
I would tag my code with the layers of the application: `http`, `business logic`, `DB access`, etc.
That way I could position the `nodes` spatially in the editor according to the tags.

this topics seems to be taboo around here so I post it on random: <https://medium.com/@stewart/we-dont-sell-saddles-here-4c59524d650d>
what are you selling that is not a bag of cool features?
the word selling may be another taboo around here too...

```We are asking a lot from our customers. We are asking them to spend hours a day in a new and unfamiliar application, to give up on years or even decades of experience using email for work communication (and abandon all kinds of ad hoc workflows that have developed around their use of email). We are asking them to switch a model of communication which defaults to public; it is an almost impossibly large ask. Almost.
To get people to say yes to a request that large, we need to (1) offer them a reward big enough to justify their effort and (2) do an exceptional, near-perfect job of execution.```
just a word clarification: even if what you are building is free you will have to "sell" it

It's just another chat app. It's a chat app with appalling UX.
Harder to relate to this sentiment when I found out that this was about Slack!
“we took IRC and wrapped it in electron with a pretty css..and oh someone else actually did the whole job for us”
I like slack because it brought back a lot of the “community” chats that died on IRC. But I dislike their technological decisions and inability to polish the product.
and :point_up::skin-tone-4: that may be the reason why in 20 years we will still be complaining about excel and python
I like the fact that we are writing this in slack
:grimacing:
wait who’s complaining about excel?
excel is the best damn enterprise data/software dev platform ever made
“An estimated 20% of genetic research papers contain errors because Excel converted some gene names into calendar dates. ” <https://twitter.com/LukasSchlogl/status/1170194419049365504>

“One study by a researcher at the University of Hawaii found that 88 percent of spreadsheets contain errors.”
<http://mrmrs.cc/writing/chaos-design/>
hahaha
well, 2nd one is research from 1998 (good article tho, thanks, didn’t read it yet), the first one is a solvable edge case.
I’m not saying it’s perfect, but I find it amazing that it’s a codeable data editor which has been used across companies, siloses and generations and everyone is familiar with it
and airtable is improving the excel experience :slightly_smiling_face:
I definitely agree with you on that, just wanted to share a couple of different viewpoints. :slightly_smiling_face:

<@UBN9AFS0N> Appreciate you trying to expose us to some thinking around product as a welcome distraction from the usual technology-focused discussions here. I wonder if there are enough people interested so we can go and hide in our own channel where we can talk less technology and more product and design?

I don’t like Slack either, but the article isn’t about Slack. It’s about a leader trying to install some product thinking into what I can only assume to be a start-up made up mostly of engineers at the time. Makes you wonder (and perhaps detect a little irony) why sending that email was thought to be useful or necessary at that time in Slack’s product life cycle.

Whether you like Slack or not, they found product-market fit and are objectively more successful than most of us here will ever be. And here’s an artifact that gives you a glimpse into how they managed to do it.

Now how do you plan to reach product-market fit? 
How does your strategy differ from “build something great and they will come”?
should we create an "adoption" channel? suggestions for a better name?

i still find it interesting that slack was just an ugly internal tool they used to help build the actual product. but the product — an actually quite fun flash game — failed and they pivoted to the tooling
docker was an internal tool by a company that provided php hosting

The founder of Slack, Stewart Butterfield, was previously co-founder of Flickr. And Flickr was also originally internal tool for a web-based game :slightly_smiling_face:.
<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flickr>
he doesn't seem to be a good game developer :stuck_out_tongue:
Perhaps it is just a sneaky strategy to hire awesome web devs to your boring b2b/b2c service startup :stuck_out_tongue:.
.. and in 20 years it'll be unavailable on the web
and it will be partly our fault for not understanding the adoption dynamics of new technologies
I was just saying it's hard to get inspired by it when it's about Slack - the sentiments expressed there are interesting, yes.

I mean actually metaprogramming: so like
```
AttemptStringConversion :: Any -> Maybe String
def AttemptStringConversion someData
perhapsCorrectFunction = Unison.Codebase.SearchBySignature((typeOf SomeData) -> String)
hopefullyString = perhapsCorrectFunction someData
switch hopefullyString 
  String -> Just hopefullyString
  _ -> Nothing
```

I mean actually metaprogramming: so like
```
AttemptStringConversion :: Any -> Maybe String
def AttemptStringConversion someData
perhapsCorrectFunction = Unison.Codebase.SearchBySignature((typeOf SomeData) -> String)
hopefullyString = perhapsCorrectFunction someData
switch hopefullyString 
  String -> Just hopefullyString
  _ -> Nothing
```

Would folks here agree that what most developers spend most of their time on is (roughly speaking) rewriting code that’s been written before instead of implementing logic truly unique to their application?

<@UHDQ62M4P> Right. Would finding good abstractions (and design constraints to adopt) solve this problem? For example, hypercard and spreadsheets are abstractions for a class of problems. They don’t do everything, but for their domains, they eliminate a vast amount of what would otherwise be perpetually reimplemented code.

<@UHDQ62M4P> Right. Would finding good abstractions (and design constraints to adopt) solve this problem? For example, hypercard and spreadsheets are abstractions for a class of problems. They don’t do everything, but for their domains, they eliminate a vast amount of what would otherwise be perpetually reimplemented code.

What I’m wondering about is whether FoC projects should be focused on more easily creating/debugging/verifying code, or more easily *reusing* code, as these may be very different (and perhaps often conflicting) goals.

What I’m wondering about is whether FoC projects should be focused on more easily creating/debugging/verifying code, or more easily *reusing* code, as these may be very different (and perhaps often conflicting) goals.

I don't think so.. the idea of perfectly re-use-able code is a pipedream: the more generic you make it to cover all possible re-use cases the less suitable it becomes for any one use, almost by definition. The design space for even simple algorithms is vast in terms of how it would work best for a particular use.

I don't think so.. the idea of perfectly re-use-able code is a pipedream: the more generic you make it to cover all possible re-use cases the less suitable it becomes for any one use, almost by definition. The design space for even simple algorithms is vast in terms of how it would work best for a particular use.
Remember, OO was one day meant to be the savior of re-use and it didn't work well.. nowadays the best kind of re-use we have is called "github repo" :wink:
Remember, OO was one day meant to be the savior of re-use and it didn't work well.. nowadays the best kind of re-use we have is called "github repo" :wink:
people will only re-use if the benefit of doing so is really vast (large functionality gained to surface area ratio), so by definition only relatively large things make sense to reuse

Yep two independent pieces of libraries won't magically work together. You have to write glue code between them. For simple things it's just easier to do the thing instead of learning two arbitrary collection of thoughts.

And for some reason ~all of my nightmare-level technical challenges I have ever encountered have been always related to 3rd party code.

And for some reason ~all of my nightmare-level technical challenges I have ever encountered have been always related to 3rd party code.
<@UHDQ62M4P> “OO was one day meant to be the savior of re-use and it didn’t work well..” Do you feel you’d be more productive reimplementing OO UI frameworks than reusing them?
<@UHDQ62M4P> “OO was one day meant to be the savior of re-use and it didn’t work well..” Do you feel you’d be more productive reimplementing OO UI frameworks than reusing them?

<@UHDQ62M4P> “people will only re-use if the benefit of doing so is really vast…” Even though most of what we do is rewrite existing code, the amount of code we write is still tiny compared to the amount of code we reuse. How many projects these days start by writing it’s own compiler, editor, OS, network stack, windowing system, UI frameworks, etc? Our apps are standing on top of a tower of abstraction giants.

<@UHDQ62M4P> “people will only re-use if the benefit of doing so is really vast…” Even though most of what we do is rewrite existing code, the amount of code we write is still tiny compared to the amount of code we reuse. How many projects these days start by writing it’s own compiler, editor, OS, network stack, windowing system, UI frameworks, etc? Our apps are standing on top of a tower of abstraction giants.

Sounds like reuse is already happening, then, by your definition? So if we're still dissatisfied, maybe the problem lies elsewhere? Is there any reason to suppose that "doing more reuse" or "doing it better" is the solution?

(I tend to be sour on reuse: <https://futureofcoding.slack.com/archives/C5T9GPWFL/p1566593111421000?thread_ts=1566430717.339900&cid=C5T9GPWFL>. But I think you may mean something different by the term than I did in that thread.)

Let's just say that I am much more productive with something like F# Suave which is somewhat lightweight FP library: web application is just a function which takes request and returns response.

Sadly I usually have to work with C# / <http://ASP.NET|ASP.NET> and I cannot honestly remember what that mammoth actually do which is net positive for productivity.
